primary_diagnosis.name: Benign peptic oesophageal stricture with acute food bolus impaction, on a background of chronic gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and suspected Barrett's oesophagus; malignant stricture not yet excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Fifteen years of gastro-oesophageal acid reflux produces chronic distal oesophagitis, which drives both columnar (Barrett's) metaplasia and progressive fibrotic scarring of the distal oesophagus — a benign peptic stricture. The narrowed lumen gives progressive dysphagia to solids; tonight a poorly chewed meat bolus has lodged at the stricture, producing acute oesophageal food bolus impaction. With the patient unable to pass even fluids, the immediate risks are aspiration of regurgitated contents, mucosal pressure necrosis, and (especially after forceful retching) oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave). The critical unexcluded alternative is an oesophageal MALIGNANCY (adenocarcinoma arising in Barrett's, or squamous cell carcinoma) presenting as a malignant stricture — clinically indistinguishable from a benign stricture without upper endoscopy and biopsy. Definitive management (endoscopic disimpaction, stricture assessment/dilatation, and biopsy) is inherently endoscopic and cannot be delivered or the diagnosis confirmed via telehealth.

differential.diagnosis: Oesophageal food bolus impaction on a benign peptic stricture
differential.evidence_basis: Acute solid-food sticking on a background of chronic reflux and recent progressive dysphagia is the classic peptic-stricture bolus picture.

differential.diagnosis: Oesophageal malignancy (adenocarcinoma / squamous cell carcinoma) presenting as malignant stricture
differential.evidence_basis: Progressive dysphagia to solids in an older adult with long-standing reflux is a cardinal alarm feature; benign and malignant strictures are indistinguishable without endoscopy + biopsy.

differential.diagnosis: Cardiac chest pain / acute coronary syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Retrosternal chest pain always warrants consideration of cardiac causes; here the pain is provoked by swallowing and accompanied by a food-sticking sensation and regurgitation, which points oesophageal — but cardiac cannot be excluded remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Oesophageal food bolus impaction on a benign peptic stricture
differential.evidence_basis: Progressive solid dysphagia over weeks + acute bolus + inability to pass fluids localises the problem to a fixed distal oesophageal narrowing.

differential.diagnosis: Oesophageal malignancy presenting as malignant stricture
differential.evidence_basis: Absence of reported weight loss or bleeding lowers but does not remove malignancy risk — endoscopy + biopsy is mandatory to exclude it.
A recognised cause of food bolus impaction, but more typical in younger atopic patients; the 15-year reflux history and age favour a peptic stricture. Distinguished on endoscopy/biopsy.
Achalasia typically causes dysphagia to solids AND liquids from onset with less prominent acid reflux; the long acid-reflux history and solid-predominant dysphagia argue against it.

differential.diagnosis: Oesophageal food bolus impaction on a benign peptic stricture (malignancy not yet excluded)
differential.evidence_basis: Able to manage saliva and no perforation features currently, but unable to tolerate fluids — an obstructing bolus needing urgent endoscopic assessment.
A time-critical complication of impaction/retching; currently screened negative (no sudden tearing chest/back pain, no dyspnoea) but must be safety-netted as it can develop.

differential.diagnosis: Acute oesophageal food bolus impaction on a peptic stricture; suspected Barrett's oesophagus; oesophageal malignancy to be excluded by endoscopy + biopsy
differential.evidence_basis: Requires urgent in-person/ED assessment for endoscopic disimpaction and mandatory endoscopic exclusion of malignancy; the diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely.

red_flags.description: Acute food bolus impaction with inability to pass oral fluids (regurgitating water)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A high-grade/complete oesophageal obstruction that will not clear risks aspiration and mucosal pressure necrosis; needs urgent endoscopic assessment/disimpaction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Aspiration pneumonia, complete obstruction, or oesophageal ischaemia/perforation if the bolus is left impacted.

red_flags.description: Progressive dysphagia to solids over weeks in an older adult with chronic reflux
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cardinal upper-GI alarm feature mandating urgent upper endoscopy to exclude oesophageal malignancy; a benign-stricture label must not be assumed without a scope and biopsy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of oesophageal cancer with stage progression and worse prognosis.

red_flags.description: Inability to swallow own saliva / drooling (pooling of secretions)
red_flags.clinical_significance: If present, indicates complete obstruction with imminent aspiration risk — an immediate emergency requiring 000. Currently absent (patient manages saliva), which supports urgent ED rather than emergency-ambulance-level triage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Airway soiling and aspiration if the transition to complete obstruction is not acted on.

red_flags.description: Sudden severe tearing chest/back pain, dyspnoea or surgical emphysema after retching — oesophageal perforation (Boerhaave)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A life-threatening complication of oesophageal impaction/forceful retching; requires immediate 000 and emergency surgical assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mediastinitis, sepsis and death if perforation is not recognised and treated urgently.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate bleeding from oesophagitis, a Mallory-Weiss tear, or an ulcerating malignancy; escalates urgency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being anchored by the 15-year 'terrible heartburn' story and reassuring the patient it is 'just reflux — take antacids', missing both the acute bolus emergency and the malignancy alarm feature.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating a benign-sounding stricture as cancer-excluded
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Assuming a benign peptic stricture and not arranging urgent endoscopy; progressive dysphagia cannot be called benign without a scope and biopsy.

Talking the patient through home bolus-clearing measures (fizzy/gas-forming drinks, more water, pushing food down) while they cannot tolerate fluids — this risks aspiration and perforation and delays endoscopic assessment.

Either fixating on cardiac pain and missing the oesophageal cause, or dismissing cardiac causes entirely; retrosternal pain warrants keeping ACS in mind even when the picture is clearly swallowing-related.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the food bolus has passed or remains impacted, and the true degree of obstruction
Oropharyngeal saliva pooling and airway status on examination
Direct visualisation of the oesophageal mucosa (salmon-pink Barrett's segment, stricture, or tumour)
Benign versus malignant stricture — requires endoscopy and biopsy
Precise blood pressure and heart rate
Signs of oesophageal perforation (surgical emphysema, mediastinal air on imaging)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person / emergency department assessment for an oesophageal food bolus that will not pass with inability to tolerate fluids, for endoscopic assessment and disimpaction. Call 000 immediately if the patient becomes unable to swallow saliva/starts drooling, develops sudden severe chest or back pain, or has difficulty breathing. After the acute episode is resolved, arrange urgent upper GI endoscopy (2-week-wait pathway) to treat the stricture and exclude malignancy.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High — the chronic GORD narrative strongly invites a benign 'just reflux' closure, which would miss the acute impaction requiring endoscopic clearance and the malignancy that only endoscopy can exclude.

guidelines_referenced.reference: GESA / BSG guidance on oesophageal food bolus impaction
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An oesophageal food bolus that does not pass, or that is associated with inability to swallow saliva, requires urgent endoscopy (within hours if secretions cannot be managed); avoid blind bolus-pushing measures.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG12 Suspected cancer recognition and referral
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: New-onset or progressive dysphagia warrants an urgent (2-week-wait) direct-access upper GI endoscopy to exclude oesophageal or gastric cancer.
Chronic GORD with a peptic stricture warrants endoscopic assessment; Barrett's metaplasia requires biopsy surveillance and any stricture requires biopsy to exclude malignancy.
